data.title: We now have a Trump Injustice Department
data.description:
I never thought I’d see the U.S. government side with the Ku Klux Klan, neo-Nazis and other violent racist groups against a prominent civil rights organization. But the Justice Department has disgracefully done this, charging the Southern Poverty Law Center with 11 counts of wire fraud, bank fraud and conspiracy to commit money laundering.

An indictment returned by a grand jury accuses the organization of paying informants at least $3 million between 2014 and 2023 to infiltrate hate groups, without disclosing the payments to its donors.

“The SPLC was not dismantling these groups,” Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche alleged. “It was instead manufacturing the extremism it purports to oppose by paying sources to stoke racial hatred.”

The Southern Poverty Law Center’s interim president and CEO, Bryan Fair, denied the ridiculous charges. “We are outraged by the false allegations,” he said, noting that for 55 years his organization “has stood as a beacon of hope fighting white supremacy and various forms of injustice to create a multi-racial democracy where we can all live and thrive.”

Fair said his organization formerly paid informants to infiltrate hate groups to gather intelligence about threats of violence and shared information it gathered with local law enforcement and the FBI. However, FBI Director Kash Patel ended his agency’s partnership with the group in October, accusing it of being “a partisan smear machine.”

Many organizations use informants to infiltrate groups suspected of criminal activity. As the Office of Inspector General for the Justice Department wrote in a 2005 report: “Since the inception of the FBI in 1908, informants have played major roles in the investigation and prosecution of a wide variety of federal crimes.”

The charges against the Southern Poverty Law Center are an example of President Trump and his administration weaponizing the Justice Department to go after groups and individuals Trump dislikes, as well as working to turn back the clock on Black progress.

For example, Trump has issued an executive order banning diversity, equity and inclusion programs; is working to make it harder to vote in a way that would disproportionately harm Black Americans; and has cut billions of dollars in funds for programs benefitting low- and moderate-income people, many of whom are Black. Trump has said civil rights laws discriminate against whites.

As a white male born into wealth and privilege, Trump has no firsthand knowledge of the systemic racism that still plagues our nation. As an African American man, I’ve experienced it multiple times and know organizations like the Southern Poverty Law Center are needed to stand against dangerous hate groups.

I first encountered violent racism in 1968, when I was six. My father, who was a civil rights attorney, was away from our home in Joliet, Ill., trying to keep peace in the streets on the night riots broke out around the nation following the assassination of the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr.

People called our home yelling racial slurs and threatening to kill our family. Some came to our home and threw rocks through our back windows, continuing racist death threats.   

Desperate to save our family, my mother gathered my two young siblings and me, grabbed the .45-caliber handgun my father kept in the house, and showed me how to fire the weapon. She told me that if attackers broke in and incapacitated her, I should grab the gun and shoot them.

I will never forget the terror I felt. Were we all about to die? Thankfully, the FBI and the police arrived to protect us and the threat ended. Many Black people have not been so fortunate, dying at the hands of racist killers. 

The Southern Poverty Law Center has saved lives by placing informants in hate groups to avert violence. The organization deserves our gratitude, not criminal charges.  

Blanche announced the charges against the group in a clear effort to impress Trump with his zeal in prosecuting Trump’s enemies. Blanche is desperately seeking to show Trump that he will be even more extreme than fired former Attorney General Pam Bondi, so he can fill her job for the rest of Trump’s term.

Under Trump, the Justice Department now investigates his enemies and seeks to come up with crimes they committed to justify prosecutions, even if no crimes occurred. This is the way “justice” works in dictatorships, not democracies.

When I was a New York state prosecutor, I always believed my duty to achieve justice was more important than winning cases. This is no longer true in the U.S. Justice Department, which should be renamed the Trump Injustice Department.

The primary job of the Trump Injustice Department is to serve as the president’s legal hit squad, forcing people targeted to devote huge amounts of time and money to defend themselves, even when charges against them are baseless.

The Southern Poverty Law Center is not the first victim of Trump’s weaponization of our federal justice system, nor will it be the last. Unfortunately, the Republican majorities in the House and Senate have ignored their responsibility to hold Trump accountable for this and many other wrongful actions. If Democrats win control of one or both chambers in the November elections, this will change. 

A. Scott Bolden is an attorney, NewsNation contributor, former chair of the Washington, D.C. Democratic Party and a former New York state prosecutor.

data.title: Republican erupts at TMZ reporter over DHS funding questions: 'Talk to the f—ing Democrats'
data.description:
Rep. Dan Meuser (R-Pa.) snapped at a reporter for TMZ who peppered him with questions about Congress’s inability to pass a Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding bill and the upcoming recess.

“It’s good to see you bro,” the video the outlet posted showed TMZ’s Charlie Cotton saying to Meuser as he was walked to the U.S. Capitol on Thursday. “I’m a bit frustrated that Congress is going on another break right now,” Cotton continued.

“You gotta keep in mind something, we work for our constituents,” Meuser said. “When we go on so-called recess, it’s district work week. I think there’s reason for perhaps staying, if we can get it done.”

Cotton continued to press Meuser on the House’s failure to pass DHS funding legislation with the Republican countering “the Democrats won’t pass legislation.”

“Stop trying to kick things up that aren’t worth it,” Meuser said, beginning to walk away.

“I’m trying to be respectful,” Cotton insisted as he continued to follow the lawmaker, to which the congressman said “you’re not trying to be respectful, don’t talk to me anymore.”

“I’m just frustrated,” Cotton continued. “We just want you to pass legislation and you guys are going home.”

“Talk to the f—— Democrats,” Meuser shot back before Cotton stopped following him.

The dust up comes as TMZ has increased its efforts in Washington, D.C., as its leadership has looked to channel frustration in Congress among the American people.

News of TMZ’s arrival has sparked both groans and curiosity among members, staffers and journalists covering Congress.

data.title: 7 House seats could shift to GOP after voting rights ruling: Cook Political Report, Sabato's Crystal Ball
data.description:
Two nonpartisan election handicappers identified seven districts that could be at risk of being redrawn to favor Republicans following the Supreme Court’s Wednesday decision limiting the scope of the Voting Rights Act provision that creates majority-minority districts.

Both Cook Political Report and Sabato’s Crystal Ball, which is published by the University of Virginia’s Center for Politics, said the court’s ruling — which deemed Louisiana’s current map an illegal racial gerrymander and ordered it be redrawn — could ultimately jeopardize all of the Democratic seats in Alabama, Mississippi, Tennessee and South Carolina.

They said it’s unclear, however, whether those effects would play out ahead of the November midterms, noting some states would be required to move candidate filing deadlines and perhaps even primary dates.

“A new map by the GOP legislature is almost certain to result in at least one Republican pickup, though it’s unclear whether that can occur in time for the 2026 election,” Amy Walter and Matthew Klein wrote in the Cook Political Report analysis. “There are still a lot of unanswered questions swirling around this decision, especially its impact on the 2026 midterm election.”

“The decision may or may not have a major, immediate impact on 2026, but its ripple effects will be felt more deeply in subsequent elections,” Kyle Kondik, managing editor of Sabato’s Crystal Ball, said in his analysis.

The handicappers identified the same seven districts that could be at risk. In Louisiana, they said, Democratic Rep. Troy Carter’s 2nd Congressional District could be targeted, alongside the 6th Congressional District, held by Rep. Cleo Fields (D), which was deemed illegal by the Supreme Court.

In Alabama, where Democrats hold two of the seven congressional districts, both Reps. Shomari Figures and Terri Sewell could be at risk if the state redrew its map.

The only Democratic representatives representing Mississippi, Tennessee and South Carolina — Rep. Bennie Thompson (Miss.), Rep. Steve Cohen (Tenn.) and Rep. Jim Clyburn (S.C.) — could also be on the chopping block, they said.

Both analyses stressed that the Supreme Court ruling will see its effect most acutely play out in elections beyond November, considering the uphill battle states would have to redraw maps in time for the midterms should they choose to proceed. But some, like Tennessee, and Louisiana have already signaled their intentions to push through new maps more expediently.

“It’s not clear that those states’ current GOP governors and legislators are eager to rush this process, which is likely to result in even more legal wrangling. They could instead punt until 2027, when the dust has settled and the political environment is less volatile,” Walter and Klein said.

“That said, pressure from the White House and other GOP leaders in their states may ultimately compel them to push through a new map by any means necessary before the midterms,” they continued, noting Tennessee has already called a special session to push through a new map.

Louisana Gov. Jeff Landry (R) signaled Thursday that the state will move forward with redistricting ahead of the November election. The Washington Post, meanwhile, reported that Landry is expected postpone the state’s May 16 primary elections.

Kondik said the coming days will likely reveal the scope of the impact on the 2026 race.

“Everyone wants to know what impact this will have on 2026,” he wrote. “The reality is that it’s hazy—we will have to see how states actually react to the decision.”

“Given the overall redistricting environment, we would expect Republicans to push hard where they have the opportunity to do so, be it for 2026 or beyond,” Kondik added. “Expect the unexpected, and don’t assume that doors that seem closed for 2026 will remain closed. We’ll know more in the coming days as states react to this ruling.”

data.title: Amazon considers 'Apprentice' reboot with Donald Trump Jr. as host
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Amazon is reportedly exploring a reboot of “The Apprentice,” with Donald Trump Jr. being considered as a possible host, according to a new report.

The original series, hosted by President Trump, aired on NBC from 2004 to 2017 and featured contestants competing for a job with the New York business mogul.

According to The Wall Street Journal, Amazon executives have held early internal discussions about reviving the show, including the idea of Trump’s eldest son stepping into the hosting role.

The report notes that conversations remain in the early stages, and Amazon has not reached out to the Trump family about the project. If it moves forward, the reboot could stream on Amazon Prime Video.

An Amazon spokesperson told The Wall Street Journal the show is not currently in active development, and no decisions have been made about a host.

“Since our acquisition of MGM, we have had preliminary internal discussions about what’s next for ‘The Apprentice’ as a property,” the spokesperson said.

Trump, already a prominent New York real estate figure, saw his national profile rise significantly during his time on the show, popularizing the catchphrase “You’re fired!” NBC cut ties with him in 2015, citing “derogatory statements” about immigrants. He was elected president the following year.

The report comes after Amazon acquired the “Melania” documentary centered on first lady Melania Trump in the lead-up to Trump’s second inauguration. The company reportedly paid $40 million for distribution rights.

Jeff Bezos, who retired as CEO of Amazon in 2021 but remains executive chairman, was asked last month if Amazon’s involvement in the documentary was part of an effort to gain favor with the Trump administration — a suggestion which he denied, according to the newsletter Status.

data.title: Sanford, former South Carolina governor, drops bid for old House seat
data.description:
Mark Sanford, the Republican former governor of South Carolina and congressman, ended his campaign for his old House seat on Thursday.

The 65-year-old, who entered the race on March 30, said in a release that he will instead launch a national nonprofit and grassroots movement focusing on tackling the national debt.

“After a lot of thought, I’ve concluded that the most effective way I can contribute right now is not by seeking office, but by helping build a broader movement focused on the country’s financial future,” he said. “The trajectory of debt and deficits isn’t a Republican problem or a Democrat problem—it’s an American problem. And it’s one that demands sustained grassroots pressure for change to occur.”

The organization, the release noted, will operate as a nonpartisan 501(c)(3) and will “focus on building a nationwide grassroots network aimed at changing our county’s current financial category.”

The current national debt is more than $38.9 trillion as of Tuesday, according to the Treasury Department. That includes more than $31.2 trillion in debt held by the public and more than $7.6 trillion in intragovernmental holdings. 

The nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office (CBO) also projected in February that the federal budget deficit will increase from $1.9 trillion in this fiscal year to $3.1 trillion in 2036. During that span, the deficit as a share of the country’s gross domestic product will rise from 5.8 percent to 6.7 percent. 

“I want to make a difference here, and as I began to get back into the world of politics it really began to hit me how all of Washington’s major changes have come as a result of outside pressure,” Sanford added. “Technical changes come from within Washington, but to do more of that on the debt issue is to rearrange chairs on the Titanic. It will take public pressure to bring lasting change.”

Sanford first represented the Palmetto State’s 1st Congressional District from 1995-2001. He then served as governor from 2003-11, before representing the district again from 2013-19.

The seat is currently held by Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), who is running in the GOP primary for South Carolina governor and is currently not seeking a fourth term in Congress. A crowded field has emerged on both sides of the aisle to succeed her.

Sanford also ran a short-lived campaign for the 2020 GOP nomination, challenging President Trump. But he dropped out of that race before a primary vote was cast.

data.title: The state race for wealth taxes will fail — just ask Europe
data.description:
From coast to coast, lawmakers are embracing wealth and millionaires’ taxes. Many of these proposals will face their ultimate test at the ballot box this November, and we should be rooting for these measures to fail.  

On the West Coast, California wants to adopt a wealth tax. Washington is greatly increasing taxes on high earners. Near the Atlantic, Rhode Island and Virginia are debating similar measures. And even in the Midwest, Michigan even debated new tax hikes this year.  

These debates are often framed in terms of fairness. Raise taxes on a few to provide benefits for most; the thinking goes. But in policy, the more important question should focus on mechanics: Will this change achieve its stated goal?

When it comes to wealth taxes, history is not on advocates’ side. Look no further than Europe for proof.

First, revenue from a wealth tax is not reliable. Revenues from net wealth taxes made up 5.12 percent of revenues in Switzerland in 2020 but just 0.19 percent of revenues in France, according to the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development. Among those five countries collecting revenues from net wealth taxes, revenues made up just 1.5 percent of total revenues on average in 2020. The reason: Wealth taxes are a nightmare to administer. 

Second, the legality of a wealth tax is still a very live question. This is true in Europe, where, in recent years, a court ruling caused the Netherlands to change course on its version of a wealth tax. It is certainly the case in the U.S. Some may argue that the 2024 ruling in Moore v. U.S. allows a narrow path for taxes on unrealized gains. However, that ruling upheld longstanding principles in corporate income taxation that do not directly translate to the wealth tax proposals we now see.  

To interpret the court’s holding in Moore as a blessing to impose any and every tax, including wealth taxes, would require an extremely broad interpretation of the 16th Amendment. These reasons alone should be enough to not even give wealth taxes a try, but for the countries that ignore these warning signs, they ultimately pay the consequences. 

An Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development report finds that wealth taxes harm entrepreneurship and risk-taking. By imposing these taxes — even on a narrow basis — countries harm innovation and long-term economic growth. This could be part of the reason so many countries have abandoned these sorts of taxes. Twelve countries in that organization imposed wealth taxes in 1996, but only five still have them today.

If states move forward with their wealth taxes, we should expect similar results. The revenue they bring in will be limited and unreliable, yet the headache they create will drive high earners and businesses out of the state.

In Washington, imposing a new tax on millionaires would bring the combined state and local top rate to over 18 percent — by far the highest in the country. Michigan’s top rate would have climbed to over 9 percent, with my organization, the Tax Foundation, estimating that it would cost 43,000 jobs, cut wages by about 1 percent, and shrink Michigan’s overall economy by $8.5 billion. The California initiative would be far from a one-time affair, and Silicon Valley may find itself with acres of empty real estate.

Two hundred fifty years ago, Adam Smith outlined four canons for taxation — among them were certainty and efficiency. The last five years in state tax policy have been marked by those principles, with dozens of states moving to institute flat or simpler income tax structures. Simpler tax codes bring in not only more revenue, but also more jobs and investment.

Sadly, the tides are turning this year, but we don’t need to repeat the mistakes of Europe. We’ve seen other countries abandon the wealth tax train; policymakers in America should avoid even getting to the station.

Daniel Bunn is president and CEO of the Tax Foundation.

data.title: Live updates: Hegseth takes heat in Senate over Pentagon leadership shakeup, Iran war
data.description:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth is back in the hot seat Thursday, facing questions from senators over the ongoing Iran war and President Trump’s $1.5 trillion defense budget request.

Hegseth gave fiery testimony in the House on Wednesday, where he clashed with Democrats and was grilled over recent Pentagon leadership firings and $400 million in withheld funds for Ukraine. The defense chief said the aid has been released.

Following Wednesday’s landmark Supreme Court decision weakening a central provision of the Voting Rights Act, Republicans and Democrats in Congress worry about its potential impact on November’s midterm elections. Both the House and Senate are in session Thursday.

Late Wednesday, Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) also cleared a key hurdle for reconciliation, adopting a budget blueprint for a second reconciliation bill that would fund immigration enforcement.

Senate Democrats are also poised for another war powers vote as the conflict in Iran nears the 60-day mark.

Trump on Thursday will host a farewell event for King Charles III and Queen Camilla before attending policy meetings.

Follow live below.

data.title: David Scott's daughter to run to fill his Georgia House seat: Report
data.description:
The daughter of the late U.S. Rep. David Scott (D-Ga.) said she will run in the special election to succeed her father. 

The Georgia lawmaker died this month, after over two decades in Congress.

Marcye Scott told The Atlanta Journal-Constitution this week that she was considering launching a political bid this cycle before her father’s death. She told the outlet she now sees the special election as a way to carry on his legacy. 

“I would love to be able to fill in the gaps for him,” she told the AJC. “I’ve got the experience — I can tell you that I know everything about the 13th District, just like him.”

“I think my father would come back to life and kick my butt if I didn’t,” she added.

Georgia’s 13th Congressional District covers portions of six counties in the Atlanta metropolitan area. The district has been a stronghold for Democrats, with the late Rep. David Scott easily defending his seat since his initial election in 2002. 

He became the first Black lawmaker to chair the House Agriculture Committee in 2021, and House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) called him a “trailblazer.” 

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp (R) has yet to schedule a special election to fill the vacant U.S. House seat. The winner of this election would serve out the remainder of the current term through the end of 2026, after which the winner of the midterms would assume office. 

State Sen. Emanuel Jones (D) is currently considered one of the top candidates in the November race, and he told the AJC that he would also consider running in the special election. 

This would not be the first time that a lawmaker’s daughter has publicly considered running for their father’s seat. 

Rep. Adelita Grijalva (D-Ariz.) won a special election last fall to represent Arizona’s 7th Congressional District, which was previously represented by her late father, former Rep. Raúl Grijalva (D-Ariz.). 

Additionally, 85-year-old Rep. Jim Clyburn (D-S.C.) told The Washington Post last month that he would like his daughter, Jennifer Clyburn-Reed, to run for his seat as he weighed a possible retirement. Clyburn later filed for reelection ahead of the state’s deadline.

data.title: WHCA shooting suspect agrees to remain detained ahead of trial
data.description:
The alleged gunman at the White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner last weekend will remain in detention ahead of trial after he agreed to be detained.

Cole Allen, 31, faces three federal charges over his alleged efforts to violently disrupt the annual black-tie dinner for journalists and public officials — including a count of attempting to assassinate President Trump. He has not yet entered a plea.   

At a hearing Thursday, Allen’s public defenders informed the court that he would not seek to challenge the government’s motion to keep him detained ahead of trial, though he reserved the right to revisit the issue in the future.  

Prosecutors asked to continue with the hearing, nonetheless, suggesting that the burden is on the government to prove detention is necessary. But the magistrate judge presiding over the hearing said it would not be necessary, calling prosecutors’ request “unprecedented.” 

“That’s a completely inefficient way of proceeding,” said Judge Moxila A. Upadhyaya. 

Prosecutors have portrayed the attack as an act of political violence, pointing to an apparent manifesto by Allen that suggested he was targeting high-ranking Trump administration officials but “would still go through most everyone here to get to the targets if it were absolutely necessary.” 

In court filings, the government urged the judge to consider the suspect’s alleged targets in weighing whether he should remain free ahead of trial, suggesting that he could have “destabilized the entire federal government” if successful because of the number of high-ranking officials at the dinner, in addition to killing or injuring other attendees.  

Allen’s public defenders had called for his release ahead of trial, pointing to his lack of criminal history and accolades including his pursuit of higher education, gainful employment and frequent church attendance.  

The lawyers wrote in court filings that Allen chose to leave nearly half of his shotgun ammunition in the hotel room, as well as a third magazine, and noted that his so-called manifesto makes no mention of Trump by name. They also emphasized the lack of clarity surrounding whether the suspect ever fired any of his weapons.  

However, they changed their tune in the courtroom after meeting with Allen. 

The attorneys also asked the judge to order the jail where he’s being held to remove him from 24-hour lockdown in a “safe cell,” but Upadhyaya declined, saying she doesn’t have the authority to make those calls for the jail.  

A different magistrate judge on Wednesday granted the defense’s request for unrestricted legal visits as his case proceeds.

Allen’s detention comes as a picture of the alleged gunman, and his goals, has started to emerge. 

In filings, prosecutors revealed that Allen took a mirror selfie roughly a half hour before the shooting. 

The photo shows the suspect wearing a black dress shirt, red tie and small leather bag “consistent in appearance with the ammunition-filled bag later recovered from his person.” The butt of a handgun can be seen from a shoulder holster, with a knife, a pair of pliers and a pair of wire cutters sheathed around his belt. 

Allen also allegedly tracked Trump’s movements ahead of the incident, visiting both the webpage “Presidential Schedule – CivicTracker” and live media coverage of his arrival at the Washington Hilton hotel for the dinner. 

That came before the prescheduled so-called manifesto was sent to Allen’s family members, friends and a former employer, titled “Apology and Explanation.” 

Minutes later, he allegedly stormed a security checkpoint in aim of breaching the ballroom where the dinner was being held.  

Allen faces charges of attempting to assassinate the president; transportation of a firearm and ammunition through interstate commerce with intent to commit a felony; and discharge of a firearm and ammunition through interstate commerce with intent to commit a felony.  

If convicted of attempting to assassinate Trump, the suspect will face a sentence of up to life in prison. The second charge carries a penalty of up to 10 years in prison, and the third charge carries a mandatory minimum of 10 years to run consecutively with any other sentence. 

U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia Jeanine Pirro, who also attended the dinner, has said additional charges are imminent as the investigation into the incident continues.  

Allen’s preliminary hearing is scheduled for May 11, if he has not yet been indicted.

data.title: Idaho lawmaker pushes 'Rat Bill' as rodent numbers surge
data.description:
(NewsNation) — An unusual but growing issue out of Idaho: A surge in rat sightings across the Boise area has prompted legislation.

State Rep. John Gannon, alongside a bipartisan group of lawmakers, introduced a late-session bill that would classify invasive rat species as a public nuisance and give local governments the authority to act. Right now, under Idaho law, cities and counties say they lack clear authority to deal with the problem.

The proposal does not mandate action or funding but instead “empowers” local governments to respond to something officials say is urgently needed as rat populations spread.

“We’ve lived here for 50 years, and this is really the first time I’ve ever heard of constant and frequent complaints about rats,” Gannon told NewsNation’s The Hill.

He described frequent complaints and firsthand accounts of rats occupying homes and garages, calling the trend unusual for the area.

It’s one thing to find rats to be annoying, but it’s another to pass legislation. Why did he go down that path?

“I think it’s a fundamental duty of government to take care of infestations such as this,” Gannon said.
